exemplar.title: How does varying the length of a water pipe (3 cm, 6 cm, 9 cm, 12 cm, and 15 cm) with a constant radius affect the flow rate of 500 mL of two different fluids, water and oil, when released from a fixed height of 15 cm above the efflux hole?

detailedExemplarData.title: How does varying the length of a water pipe (3 cm, 6 cm, 9 cm, 12 cm, and 15 cm) with a constant radius affect the flow rate of 500 mL of two different fluids, water and oil, when released from a fixed height of 15 cm above the efflux hole?

subject.title: Physics
subject.description:
**IB Physics** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **space, time and motion; the particulate nature of matter; wave behaviour; fields; and nuclear and quantum physics**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, data analysis, and scientific investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice questions and data-based questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 1 hour 30 minutes | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Short-answer and extended-response questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 2 hours 15 minutes | 50% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Individual scientific investigation | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Rawâdata and processedâdata tables are clearly labeled with appropriate units and propagated uncertainties.
Graphs are properly titled, include bestâfit equations, and demonstrate correct linearization (Qâ1/L).
Sample calculation for Q=V/t is clearly shown, aiding transparency of processing steps.
weaknesses: Errorâbar treatment is dismissed without numerical justification, and combined uncertainty on the gradient is omitted.
Some table headers lack explicit units (e.g., flowârate units) leading to potential ambiguity.
The method for determining length uncertainty (Â±5Ã10â»Â³ m) is not explained.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Data recording and processing are communicated clearly, and calculations are accurate and consistent. However, omissions in uncertainty discussion and label clarity limit the mark to the top of the 3â4 band.

strengths: The research question is embedded in a concrete realâworld context (village water pipes) and directly linked to Poiseuilleâs law.
Specificity in pipe length and choice of two fluids (water and oil) enhances relevance and clarity.
Apparatus list includes uncertainties and stepâbyâstep instructions allow replication with only minor queries.
weaknesses: Procedure omits a cleaning protocol and bubbleâremoval steps that could affect reproducibility.
Hypothesis remains qualitative; a predicted numeric gradient would strengthen testability.
Controlâvariable table lacks a Reynoldsânumber check to justify laminar flow assumptions.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The hypothesis is situated in a highly specific context and the methodology is detailed enough for nearâseamless reproduction, warranting a high mark. Minor procedural omissions prevent a perfect score, but the clear, precise contextual framing and largely reproducible method justify a bandâ5 award.

strengths: Conclusion correctly states the inverse proportionality of flow rate to pipe length and ties it to a high RÂ² value.
Discussion refers back to Poiseuilleâs law and notes the gradient difference between water and oil.
A relevant CFD study is cited, linking findings to accepted scientific literature.
weaknesses: Mixâup in describing which fluid is more viscous contradicts known properties.
No quantitative comparison between experimental and theoretical gradients is provided.
Units in the conclusion text are inconsistently stated (e.g., mÂ³/s vs Ã10â»â¶ mÂ³/s).
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion is largely consistent with the analysis and justified by highâquality graph data. Minor interpretative errors and lack of quantitative theory comparison prevent reaching the highest band.

strengths: Specific methodological weaknesses (turbulence, timing error, air resistance) are identified.
Realistic improvements (flow regulator, automated timing sensor, airflow control) are proposed.
The improvements are relevant and tailored to each identified limitation.
weaknesses: The relative impact of each methodological weakness on the overall results is not quantified.
Explanations for how much each improvement would reduce uncertainty remain brief.
No discussion of cost or feasibility tradeâoffs for the proposed improvements.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation describes specific limitations and offers realistic, relevant improvements, aligning with the top of the 3â4 band. Lack of depth in impact analysis prevents a bandâ5 rating.

strandSelections.reasoning: The research question is not only clearly stated but is also embedded in a concrete real-world anecdote (village water pipes) and firmly linked to Poiseuilleâs law.  The student explains why pipe length and two specific fluids are worth studying, showing precise and appropriate context rather than a broad, generic backdrop.  Minor stylistic issues do not undermine the specificity provided, so the strand comfortably fits the 5â6 band; a mid-band score of 5 is most representative.

Instrumental uncertainties are stated, a standard-deviation repeatability is calculated, and a brief propagation for 1/L is included.  However, the student dismisses error bars as ânegligibleâ without numerical comparison, does not account for timing reaction error, and omits combined uncertainty on gradient.  The consideration is present but contains notable omissions, matching the middle of the 3â4 band.

Control variables are tabulated and their purposes stated, and preliminary trials justify the choice of timing the full 500 mL outflow.  The student notes laminar flow concerns, turbulence, and temperature control, yet the discussion stops short of explaining how each consideration ensures that sufficient, relevant data will answer the research question (e.g., no quantitative justification for five trials, no Reynolds-number check).  Thus methodological considerations are described but not fully explained, placing the work solidly in the 3â4 band at a score of 3.

Three distinct weaknesses are identified (turbulence, human timing error, air resistance) and each is described with a short discussion of error type.  The relative impact on results (e.g., how turbulence alters Q vs 1/L) is not analysed in depth, so the work meets the descriptor for âdescribes specific methodological weaknessesâ rather than fully explaining their impact.

The procedure lists apparatus with uncertainties, sets out step-by-step instructions, and identifies how fluid level is set, how time is measured, and how each pipe is exchanged.  Someone replicating the work could do so with only minor questions (e.g., exact inner diameter verification, cleaning protocol details).  These small ambiguities keep it from the seamless reproducibility required for the 5â6 band, but the description still allows reproduction with few omissions, meriting the top of the 3â4 band.

Flow rates are correctly derived from volume and time for every trial, means and standard deviations are calculated, and linearization (Q vs 1/L) is employed appropriately with best- and worst-fit gradients.  Apart from a minor unit slip in the conclusion text, computations are accurate and internally consistent, satisfying the 5â6 band; a score of 5 reflects small execution slips that fall short of perfect accuracy.

For each listed weakness, the student proposes a realistic improvement (flow regulator, automated sensor, airflow control).  These suggestions are relevant and go beyond simple restatement, yet the explanations remain brief and lack quantitative justification of how much the improvement would reduce uncertainty.  Thus improvements are described rather than fully explained, aligning with the 3â4 band at a score of 3.

Raw-data tables carry units and uncertainties, processed-data tables show means and propagated errors, and graphs are properly titled with axes and best-fit equations.  Although some formatting is messy, the information is conveyed both clearly and with adequate numerical precision.  This meets the descriptor for clear and precise communication, warranting the upper level of the 3â4 band.

The report references Poiseuilleâs law and cites a CFD study to place findings in accepted scientific context.  However, the comparison remains qualitative; no theoretical flow-rate values or viscosity ratios are calculated for a rigorous check.  Hence there is some relevant comparison but it is not fully developed, positioning the strand in the middle of the 3â4 band.

The student states that flow rate is inversely proportional to pipe length and supports this with a high RÂ² value and gradient discussion, tying the numerical result to Poiseuilleâs law.  Minor inconsistencies (momentary mix-up about which fluid is more viscous) and lack of quantitative comparison to theoretical gradient prevent full alignment with analysis, but overall the conclusion is largely consistent and justified, warranting the top of the 3â4 band.

annotations.comment: The anecdotal introduction effectively grounds the research question in a realâworld scenario, enhancing motivation and context.
Background could be deepened by mentioning how non-Newtonian fluids deviate from Poiseuilleâs law to broaden theoretical context.
The hypothesis is qualitative; consider adding an expected numeric gradient from theory to make it testable.
In the variables table, include a Reynolds number check under controls to justify laminar flow conditions.
The research question is clearly stated with specific lengths and fluids, situating the inquiry in a precise context linked to Poiseuilleâs law.
The title could specify both fluids (water and oil) under study to immediately signal the comparative scope.
Column header âÃ10â»Â² Â±0.0005 mâ is unclearâspecify that 3, 6, etc., represent 3Ã10â»Â² m for reader clarity.
The sample calculation using Q=V/t is presented clearly with units, aiding comprehension of processing steps.
Table 6 omits units in the flow-rate header; include âÃ10â»â¶ mÂ³/sâ explicitly to avoid ambiguity.
Processed Data (Table 7) clearly presents mean Q values with uncertainties, facilitating precise interpretation.
How was the L uncertainty (Â±5Ã10â»Â³ m) determined? Provide methodology or instrument specs.
Graph 1 statement dismisses error bars despite their presence in data; include them to maintain transparency.
The control-variable table omits a cleaning protocol between testing water and oil, which is essential for reproducibility.
The risk assessment is thorough in scope, addressing safety, environmental and ethical concerns comprehensively.
Quantify the expected volume of waste and reuse rates to strengthen environmental mitigation details.
Apparatus list omits uncertainty for pipe length and radius; this should be stated to support error propagation.
Include cleaning solution and drying time in materials to ensure repeatable fluid properties.
Procedure does not specify how air bubbles in the pipe are removed, yet they can alter flow measurements.
Reformat nested step numbering for clarity to ensure each action is unambiguous.
Raw data are recorded clearly in Table 5 with appropriate units for time and trials.
Acknowledgment of high RÂ² (~0.9993) and negligible error bars shows strong grasp of fit quality.
The conclusion should include a quantitative comparison between experimental and theoretical gradients to deepen the scientific context.
Conclusion references R value correctly but misstates flow units (âmÂ³/sâ vs âÃ10â»â¶ mÂ³/sâ); verify unit consistency.
Evaluation lists specific limitations but omits explanation of their relative impact on overall result accuracy.
The linearization equation Qâ1/L is correctly applied, demonstrating solid understanding of proportional reasoning.
Add RÂ² values and best-fit equations directly on Graph 2 to enhance clarity of the linear relationship.
Worstâfit line graphs lack legends for water and oil series; add clear keys to differentiate data sets.
Include gradient uncertainty (Â±) and RÂ² on the worstâfit lines to illustrate data spread and fit quality.
Analysis misinterprets the gradient difference to claim water has greater viscosity than oil, contradicting known physical properties.
Mitigation strategies offered are realistic (e.g., flow sensor, pressure regulator), demonstrating thoughtful improvement planning.
Further scope correctly proposes laminarâturbulent transition and new fluid types, offering a clear path for extended investigation.
Consider specifying candidate non-Newtonian fluids (e.g., glycerin, honey) with viscosity ranges to sharpen further-scope proposals.

rubricData.rubricSpecificInfo: âThe Physics Internal Assessment (IA) is a critical component of the IB Diploma Programme, providing students with the opportunity to design and conduct independent investigations into physics phenomena. This assessment emphasizes the development of practical skills, analytical thinking, and a deep understanding of scientific methodologies. By engaging in the IA, students not only apply theoretical knowledge to real-world scenarios but also cultivate competencies in research design, data analysis, and critical evaluation, all of which are essential for academic and professional success in scientific fields. â

similarExemplars.title:
How does the length of the pipe affect the natural frequency of the air column in the pipe at constant air temperature when the pipe is:
a)	open at both ends and air is blown close to one end?
b)	open at one and closed at the other end, and air is blown close to the open end?
Natural frequency is the frequency that has the highest intensity and is the loudest of all frequencies which are produced with air blowing.
